pattern: The longest chain through a fixed point splits cleanly into the longest chain endable strictly before it and the longest chain startable strictly after it, and each half is a classic two-dimensional longest-increasing-subsequence.
hints: Split the points into those strictly below-and-left of the target point and those strictly above-and-right of it -- only those two groups can ever sit before or after the target in a valid chain.

Within either group, finding the longest chain is exactly the '2D longest increasing subsequence' problem: sort by x ascending (breaking ties by y descending so equal-x points can't chain together), then run the standard patience-sorting LIS on the y-values.
The answer is 1 (the target itself) plus the longest chain found in the before-group plus the longest chain found in the after-group.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, chain through the middle point
a single point, trivially a chain of length 1
all points form one strictly increasing chain
k is the first index, nothing can precede it
k is the last index, nothing can follow it
points sharing the target's x or y coordinate must be excluded from both groups
before-group has ties in x that must not chain together

stress.description: random distinct points with a fixed target index
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(500, size))
    seen = set()
    coords = []
    while len(coords) < n:
        p = (rng.randint(0, 2 * n), rng.randint(0, 2 * n))
        if p not in seen:
            seen.add(p)
            coords.append(list(p))
    k = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
    return [coords, k]
